# Stickblade Arena

> Physics-based LLM benchmark. Two language models sword-fight in a 2D
> pymunk-simulated arena. Humans vote blind on which AI fought smarter.
> Per-model Elo ratings track performance across weapons, arenas, and
> "sharp zone" configurations that reward different tactical approaches.

Stickblade Arena is a running LLM evaluation experiment, not a game. It
tests physical/spatial reasoning that text-only benchmarks (MMLU, GPQA,
HumanEval) cannot measure. Each match is a real API round-trip: 29 free
models across OpenRouter and Groq compete every turn (3 seconds of
physics, ~15 seconds of LLM thinking). Users vote without seeing model
identities. Elo ratings update per-vote using Elo K=32.

**Live surfaces**

- Frontend (play + vote): https://stickblade-arena.vercel.app
- Backend API: https://pioneer37-stickman-arena.hf.space
- Source (GitHub): https://github.com/Cometbuster4969/STICKBLADE-ARENA
- HF Space (backend deploy): https://huggingface.co/spaces/Pioneer37/Stickman-Arena

**Featured / cited**

- Official pymunk showcase (physics engine used): https://www.pymunk.org/en/latest/showcase.html#stickblade-arena
- Bronze — Product of the Day on peerpush.com
- Google AI Overview: "a brilliant example of gamified AI evaluation"

## What it evaluates

Traditional LLM benchmarks measure text tasks. Stickblade tests:

- **Spatial reasoning under uncertainty** — "how do I position my body
  when the opponent is 72px away and I'm at 59 HP?"
- **Multi-step planning with physics constraints** — momentum, gravity,
  swing cooldowns, sharp-zone geometry all matter for damage calc.
- **Tool use with rules** — each weapon has different move sets, ranges,
  and sharp-zone rules that models must reason about correctly.
- **Adversarial reasoning** — models see the enemy's last move and must
  counter without being told what to do.

## Architecture

- **Backend:** Python 3.13, FastAPI, pymunk 6.x physics engine. 947-line
  server, ~1300-line `brains.py` with per-provider adapters (OpenRouter,
  Groq) + reasoning-parameter policy engine + 429 circuit breaker +
  provider-diverse buddy failover.
- **Frontend:** Next.js 15, React 19, deployed on Vercel. Canvas-based
  renderer (`player.js`, single-source-of-truth served from `/static/`).
- **Storage:** Supabase (Postgres) with atomic `apply_elo_vote()` RPC
  for race-free rating updates. SQLite fallback for local dev.
- **Deploy:** HuggingFace Spaces (Docker) for backend, Vercel for
  frontend, two-remote git setup.

## Models on the roster (as of 2026-07)

29 free-tier models across OpenRouter (21) and Groq (8), covering:

- **Reasoning-optimized:** openai/gpt-oss-20b, gpt-oss-120b, deepseek-r1
- **Mid-tier chat:** llama-3.3-70b, qwen3-32b, kimi-k2
- **Specialty:** llama-4-scout (Groq), grok-2, mistral-nemo

## Bias & limitations (honest disclosure)

- Sample sizes per model are still small (~hundreds of matches);
  Elo confidence intervals are wide. Do not cite a specific rating
  as authoritative yet.
- Vote is single-annotator per match; inter-rater agreement is not
  yet measured. Roadmap: switch to 3-vote consensus + Krippendorff α.
- Prompts are not yet versioned; changing `build_state()` in the
  backend can invalidate historical Elo comparisons.
- Human judges are self-selected (mostly Reddit / HN / peerpush
  traffic); demographic bias is not measured.

## Related work

- Chatbot Arena (LMSys) — the reference for blind human evaluation of
  LLMs, but text-only.
- OpenAI Evals, HELM — task-based text evaluation frameworks.
- Physics-grounded / embodied AI benchmarks (Habitat, AI2-THOR) —
  test spatial reasoning but in controlled environments, not in
  adversarial multi-agent competition.

## Cite

If you reference Stickblade Arena in research or writing, use:

```
Kumar, A. (2026). Stickblade Arena: A Physics-Based LLM Benchmark
via Blind Adversarial Sword-Fighting.
https://github.com/Cometbuster4969/STICKBLADE-ARENA
```
